assumptions: The intragroup dividend has been declared and recognised as an intercompany receivable/payable at the reporting date.
The analysis is limited to hedge accounting eligibility in consolidated financial statements under the provided IFRS 9 and IFRIC 16 excerpts.
The relevant issue is foreign currency risk on the intercompany balance, not a hedge of the underlying investment in the subsidiary.
Different functional currencies may exist within the group; if they do not, the specific foreign currency hedge exception would not arise.

approaches.label: General intragroup hedged-item prohibition
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items generally must be with parties external to the reporting entity. An intragroup dividend receivable is internal to the group.
approaches.practical_implication: Formal documentation alone is insufficient unless a specific IFRS 9 exception is available.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidation to external-party assets, liabilities, firm commitments or highly probable forecast transactions.

approaches.label: Intragroup monetary item foreign currency hedge
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 permits foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item, such as a receivable/payable, to qualify in consolidated financial statements if the FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: the dividend is recognised as an intragroup monetary receivable/payable
the entities involved have different functional currencies
the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
the hedge relationship is formally designated and documented and meets hedge effectiveness requirements
approaches.practical_implication: A consolidated hedge relationship may be possible if the declared dividend receivable creates residual consolidated FX exposure.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item to qualify as a hedged item in consolidation when residual FX exposure remains.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation, an economic relationship, and an appropriate hedge ratio.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction cash flow hedge
approaches.reasoning: The intragroup forecast-transaction exception applies only to highly probable forecast transactions whose foreign currency risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. A dividend already recognised as a receivable is no longer a forecast transaction.
approaches.practical_implication: This route is relevant only before recognition and only if the FX risk will flow through consolidated profit or loss.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions to be hedged in consolidation.
Paragraphs B6.3.5 and B6.3.6 require the forecast intragroup transaction's foreign currency risk to affect consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge alternative
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 addresses hedges of net investments in foreign operations, where the hedged item is an amount of net assets of the foreign operation. It should not be applied by analogy to other hedge types such as an intercompany dividend receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not treat a dividend receivable hedge as a net investment hedge unless the exposure being hedged is actually the qualifying net investment.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 11 and 12 define the hedged item as an amount of net assets of the foreign operation and the hedged risk as FX exposure between functional currencies.
Paragraph 8 states the Interpretation applies only to hedges of net investments and should not be applied by analogy to other hedge accounting relationships.
